text: Here, the patient is lying on a flat bed.
If left in this position, the hip will impede downward movement of the thoracoscope.
Thus, it is important to maximally flex the bed to allow free movement of the thoracoscope.
We use three incisions, a 2 cm posterior incision, a 2 cm anterior camera port,
and a four centimeter utility incision.
Incisions are placed under thoracoscopic visualization.
For this right-sided lesion, a camera port has been placed at the anterior axillary line, eighth inner space.
The utility incision is placed directly over the superior pulmonary vein for upper lobectomies
and one inner space lower for middle or lower lobectomies.
We place the posterior port at the edge of the lower lobe base.
A ring clamp is used to manipulate the lung.
And by digital palpation, we identify the lesion, in this case, in the right upper lobe.
The procedure is then performed from anterior to posterior.
We will now demonstrate a right upper lobectomy in its entirety.
Through the utility incision, forceps and a long Metzenbaum scissors are used to dissect the superior pulmonary vein.
A harken clamp is then passed behind the superior pulmonary vein
One must also identify and spare the right middle lobe vein.
And a monofilament suture is passed to aid in retraction.
Then, through the posterior port, an endoscopic stapler is passed behind the vein.
The suture is removed, the stapler is closed, and the vein is transected.
This exposes the main pulmonary artery.
The truncus branch is then dissected.
A harken clamp is then passed behind the artery.
A monofilament suture is passed.
Then the stapler, again from the posterior port, is placed behind the artery, and the artery is then transected.
This then exposes the right upper lobe bronchus.
A harking clamp is used to dissect behind the bronchus.
Then a universal stapler is applied through the posterior port onto the bronchus.
The bronchus is then transected.
This exposes the recurrent branch of the pulmonary artery.
Again through the posterior port, the recurrent branch is transected with the vascular stapler.
Once all the lobar structures have been divided, the fissures are then assessed.
Ring forceps are used to manipulate the upper and lower lobes.
An endoscopic stapler divides the major and minor fissures via the utility incision.
Once the lobe is free, a heavy lap sack is placed in the chest, angled towards the apex, and the lobe is placed within the lap sack.
The specimen is then pulled through the 4 centimeter utility incision.
As you can see, this incision is only twice as large as the thoracoscopic port.
The pleura bounded by the superior vena cava and the trachea is incised.
The nodal packet is dissected.
The azagus vein has already been divided for exposure.
The nodal packet is then grasped using a curved ring clamp.
Just as in the right upper lobe, the left upper lobe vein is identified.
The endovascular stapler through the posterior port divides the vein.
This exposes the first apical branch of the pulmonary artery, which should be transected next.
Once it is identified and transected, this will provide adequate room to encircle the left upper lobe bronchus.
A harking clamp is then passed behind the left upper lobe bronchus
care taken not to injure the posterior pulmonary artery.
Once this is encircled, a universal stapler is passed through the posterior port and the bronchus is transected.
The remaining arterial branches are divided and the fissure is completed.
The mediastinal pleura is incised.
The lymph node packet is then grasped with a curved ring clamp.
Metzenbaum scissors and electrocautery are then used to dissect the lymph nodes off the pulmonary artery and the aorta.
A left-sided level 7 nodal dissection has already been completed.

One should note the inferior pulmonary vein, the esophagus, the left main stem bronchus and carina, and the pericardium, which is now easily visualized now that the level 7 lymph node packet has been removed.
